messages.content: is this considered as search based software engineering “The world is growing and developing rapidly, and the demand for software has been increasing speedily, any software has many steps for building a program and all the steps are important for software requirements. Requirements classification can be applied manually, which requires great effort, time, cost and the accuracy may vary. Therefore, many previous researchv has been proposed to automate the classification process, but the automation process of the classification was not sufficient. In this study, we will propose a technique to automatically classify software requirements using machine learning to represent text data from software requirements specification and classify requirement to group Functional Requirement and Non-Functional Requirement. The experimented dataset in this study was the PROMISE_exp, which includes labeled requirements. All the documents of software from the database were changed (cleaned) with a set of steps (normalization, extractions, selection any techniques that will be used. The BoW used SVM algorithm or KNN algorithm for classification. This study used data from the PROMISE_exp to do the work, the information of the steps used to re-performed the classification, and the Measurement BoW, when using SVM and KNN algorithms the classification of requirements making can serve as a way and resources for another study. It can be seen that the use of BoW with SVM is better than use KNN algorithms with an average F-measure of all cases of 0.74. In future work we intend to improve to technique with make merge and change some algorithms as Logiest Regression to improve the Accuracy ( Precision) of our model.”

Yes, this can be considered as search-based software engineering. The study aims to automate the classification process of software requirements using machine learning techniques, specifically using Bag of Words (BoW) with SVM and KNN algorithms. The goal is to improve the accuracy and precision of the classification model.

is this considered as search based software engineering "With the expansion of the scale of software projects and the number of users, excessive software requirements are repeating and overlapping one another. It is getting more fundamental and important to sort the requirements according to their priority using the technologies of software requirements prioritization for costs saving and demands satisfying. This paper analyzed the merits and demerits of the technologies of software requirements prioritization based on sorting and searching, summarized and discussed some important problems demanding prompt solution, related to requirements clustering and redundancy elimination, customers clustering and requirements prioritization based on customer groups, the management of the dependencies between requirements."

Yes, this can be considered as search-based software engineering. The paper discusses the importance of sorting software requirements based on priority using software requirements prioritization techniques. It specifically mentions technologies based on sorting and searching, which are common approaches in search-based software engineering. The paper analyzes different methods for requirements clustering, redundancy elimination, customer clustering, and prioritization based on customer groups. It also addresses the management of dependencies between requirements, which is a relevant aspect in search-based software engineering.
is their a field called design testing in software engineering?

Yes, there is a field called design testing in software engineering. Design testing involves evaluating the design of software systems to ensure they meet specific criteria and requirements. It focuses on verifying that the design elements and architectural decisions are implemented correctly, and testing them against design specifications. Design testing helps identify design flaws, inconsistencies, and potential risks in the software design before the actual coding and implementation phase. It is an important component of software quality assurance and plays a crucial role in ensuring the reliability, maintainability, and scalability of software systems.
what are the sub fields of software testing of software engineering

There are several subfields or specialized areas within software testing in software engineering. Some of the common subfields include:

1. Functional Testing: Verifies whether the software functions as intended and meets the specified functional requirements.

2. Performance Testing: Evaluates the performance characteristics of the software, such as response time, scalability, and resource usage.

3. Security Testing: Assesses the security vulnerabilities and weaknesses of the software, including testing for authentication, authorization, encryption, and protection against malicious attacks.

4. Usability Testing: Focuses on the user experience and interface, ensuring that the software is user-friendly and intuitive.

5. Regression Testing: Re-tests previously tested functionalities to ensure that changes or enhancements have not caused unintended side effects or regressions.

6. Integration Testing: Checks the interaction and compatibility between different modules or components of the software system.

7. System Testing: Verifies the software as a whole to ensure that all integrated components function correctly together.

8. Acceptance Testing: Conducted by users or clients to determine whether the software meets their specific requirements and expectations.

9. Non-functional Testing: Testing for non-functional aspects such as reliability, maintainability, portability, and compatibility.

10. Test Automation: Utilizing tools and frameworks to automate the testing process, allowing for faster and more efficient execution of tests.

These are just some examples of the subfields within software testing, and there may be more specialized areas depending on the specific needs and requirements of a software project.
depending on the previous explanation, what is this filed "using gamification in software testing exercises to measure the engagement and performance of students "

The field you mentioned, "using gamification in software testing exercises to measure the engagement and performance of students," can be considered as an application or specialized approach within software testing. It combines the principles of gamification with software testing exercises to enhance student engagement and measure their performance.

Gamification refers to the use of game-like elements, such as challenges, rewards, and competitions, in non-game contexts to motivate and engage participants. In the context of software testing, gamification can be applied by creating interactive and immersive testing scenarios, incorporating leaderboards, badges, or rewards for achieving certain testing goals, and providing real-time feedback on performance.

The use of gamification in software testing exercises for students aims to make the learning process more enjoyable, stimulating, and interactive. It can encourage students to actively participate in testing activities, apply their knowledge and skills in a practical setting, and compete or collaborate with their peers. The engagement and performance of students can be measured using various metrics, such as completion rates, bug detection, time taken to complete tasks, or overall scores.

By incorporating gamification into software testing exercises, educators can create an environment that not only teaches core testing concepts and techniques but also enhances student motivation, retention, and overall learning outcomes.
what is the main area and sub area

The main area of the field you mentioned is "gamification in software testing exercises." This involves integrating gamification principles and strategies into the context of software testing to enhance engagement and measure the performance of students.

The subarea within this field could be described as "measuring the engagement and performance of students." This focuses specifically on evaluating and quantifying the level of student engagement and their performance in software testing exercises that incorporate gamification elements. It may involve identifying appropriate metrics, designing assessment frameworks, and analyzing the data collected to gain insights into student engagement and performance.

Overall, the main area is gamification in software testing exercises, while the subarea is centered around measuring student engagement and performance.

proofreading "Additionally, GoRace, a gamification tool, was used by students to conduct Olympic race between two locations were they rewarded with higher distance units and divine points based on the assessment of test suites effectiveness"

Additionally, GoRace, a gamification tool, was used by students to conduct an Olympic race between two locations. They were rewarded with higher distance units and divine points based on the assessment of test suite effectiveness.

make it shorter "After informing students about the experiment and its procedures, demographic information was collected through a questionnaire. The duration of the exercises was 3 hours in face-to-face setting and 12 hours of homework, except for the first exercise which has one hour homework. Each exercise consisted of five activities: 1) test design, where a class explained the specification and designed the tests; 2) teams applied strategies to understand the input of each test; 3) students individually performed a test suite to provide feedback on effectiveness and improve it. 4) discussion of alternatives and their effectiveness; 5) based on that, students improve their test suite and execute it in SQLTest. Then, the information was sent directly to GoRace to track their progress. A similar procedure was applied to the control group without collecting demographic information or using GoRace tool."

Having informed students about the experiment and its procedures, demographic information was collected through a questionnaire. The exercises lasted for 3 hours in face-to-face sessions and 12 hours as homework, except for the first exercise, which required one hour of homework. Each exercise comprised five activities: test design, input understanding strategies, individual test suite performance and feedback, discussion of alternatives, and improvement of the test suite followed by execution in SQLTest. Progress tracking was done through GoRace. The control group underwent a similar process, but without demographic data collection or the use of the GoRace tool.

proofreading and make it shorter "To answer RQ1 null hypothesis were formulated which is no difference between the tow groups in the level of engagement. To test it, the four metrics were analyzed using statistical analysis (mean, median, Mann-Whiteny U test and effect sizes for individual exercises and for Olympic race as total. It is noticeable that, the mean of the experimental group is greater than the controlled group. However, the individual results present the same significant differences except in the last exercise which slightly higher for the control group. According to p-value, it has been found that there is significant difference between the two groups in the Number of Test Execution and the Active Time while no differences in the other two metrics. Additionally, in the experiment group the participation rates were higher and Dropout rate were lower than the controlled group. The participation rate in the experimental group is higher than the controlled group in the first three exercises while no difference were in Dropout Rate. That highlights the finding from Olympic Race is inline with the first three exercises which the experimental group work more and drop less than the other group. Based on that the null hypothesis is rejected and the engagement of the experimental group is higher than the control group."

To address RQ1 and test the null hypothesis stating that there is no difference in engagement levels between the two groups, statistical analysis was conducted on four metrics using mean, median, Mann-Whitney U test, and effect sizes. It is worth noting that the mean of the experimental group was higher than the control group. Individual results consistently showed significant differences, except for the last exercise where the control group had a slightly higher score. The analysis revealed significant differences in the Number of Test Execution and Active Time metrics, while no differences were found in the other two metrics. Additionally, the experimental group exhibited higher participation rates and lower dropout rates compared to the control group. The participation rate was higher in the experimental group for the first three exercises, with no difference in the Dropout Rate. This aligns with the findings from the Olympic Race, indicating that the experimental group had higher engagement and lower dropout rates. Thus, the null hypothesis is rejected, confirming that the engagement of the experimental group is higher than the control group.

proofreading and make short "To address RQ2, a null hypothesis were stated that there is no difference between the two groups in performance. The same analytics approach were followed on two metrics: Effectiveness and Increase Effectiveness. The results for Olympic Race from p-value indicate significant differences between the two groups. The mean of both metrics is higher in experimental group than the control group which contradict the hypothesis. Regarding the analysis of each exercise, it has been found that the performance of the experimental group was higher in all the exercises. As a result, null hypothesis is rejected and RQ1 was answered that the performance of students with gamification experience is better than the students with non-gamified experience."

To address RQ2, a null hypothesis was formulated stating that there is no difference in performance between the two groups. Two metrics, Effectiveness and Increase Effectiveness, were analyzed using the same approach. The p-values for the Olympic Race showed significant differences between the two groups. The mean values for both metrics were higher in the experimental group, contradicting the hypothesis. In the analysis of each exercise, the performance of the experimental group was consistently higher. Consequently, the null hypothesis is rejected, and RQ2 is answered affirmatively - students with gamification experience performed better than those without gamification experience.

proofreading "Strengths points in the paper:
Formulate null hypothesis to answer research questions consider as a good practice as it helps to conduct clear framework for analysing the results.
Analysing overall results (Olympic Race) and individual exercise results provide detailed assessment of the engagement and performance.
The presentation of the results using tables provide a comprehensive overview of the findings.
"

Strengths of the paper:
- The formulation of null hypotheses to address research questions is considered a good practice as it provides a clear framework for analyzing the results.
- Analyzing both the overall results (Olympic Race) and individual exercise results offers a detailed assessment of engagement and performance.
- The presentation of results using tables provides a comprehensive overview of the findings.

proofreading this "There is no clear explanation of how data collection process was conducted specially in control group. While the authors explained the way of SQLTest and GoRace worked, there is lack of details on how metrics obtained and calculated the data in control group and how it relates to the effectiveness of the test suites.
There is a potential confirmation bias in presenting the positivity in conducting gamification in software engineering without discussing any potential limitations or drawbacks. 
"

There is no clear explanation of how the data collection process was conducted, especially in the control group. While the authors explained the functioning of SQLTest and GoRace, there is a lack of detail regarding how metrics were obtained and how the data in the control group was calculated, as well as its relevance to the effectiveness of the test suites.

Additionally, there is a potential confirmation bias in presenting the benefits of gamification in software engineering without discussing any potential limitations or drawbacks.
